---
title: "ConnectTrack: Pipeline Design"
author:
  - name: Chioma Thornton
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0000
    email: thornton@coranefarm.org
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - Corane Farm Research
  - name: Fatima Al-Rashidi
    affiliations:
      - Corane Farm Research
date: 2026-01-15
abstract: |
  This document describes the design rationale and specifications for ConnectTrack,
  a pipeline for reconstructing and tracking synaptic connectivity across
  developmental time points using serial FIB-SEM and automated segmentation.
---

## Introduction

ConnectTrack aims to enable longitudinal connectomic analysis of sensorimotor circuits during postnatal development. This document covers the design decisions made during development of the acquisition and reconstruction pipeline.

## Design overview

The pipeline has three stages: (1) tissue preparation and FIB-SEM acquisition, (2) automated neuron segmentation and synapse detection, and (3) connectivity graph construction and temporal alignment across developmental ages.

::: {.callout-note}
## Design files
CAD files, microscope configuration scripts, and segmentation model weights are available at [link to repository or file share].
:::

## Components

| Component | Specification | Supplier | Part number |
|-----------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| FIB-SEM system | 4 nm isotropic voxels | Zeiss | Crossbeam 550 |
| Vibratome | 50 µm sections | Leica | VT1200S |
| GPU cluster | 8× A100, 80 GB | NVIDIA | DGX A100 |
| Storage | 2 PB object storage | Corane Farm Research IT | — |

: Key components used in the ConnectTrack pipeline. {#tbl-components}

## Design considerations

### Trade-off 1: voxel size vs. volume

Higher resolution (4 nm voxels) fully resolves thin axons and small synapses but limits the acquirable volume per session to ~50 × 50 × 50 µm³. We accept this trade-off because the sensorimotor target regions are compact and the synaptic ultrastructure detail is essential for identifying synapse type and maturation state.

### Trade-off 2: automated vs. proofreading-assisted segmentation

Fully automated segmentation at scale is necessary given data volumes, but introduces merge and split errors. We apply a confidence-weighted proofreading strategy, prioritizing manual correction for edges with low affinity scores rather than exhaustive review, achieving >95% synapse detection recall at roughly one-tenth the proofreading cost of full manual reconstruction.

## Version

Document version: {{< meta git-version >}}
